section.title: Witness Against Israel
section.sectionRationale: The first of the sermons of judgment. Amos defends his right to prophesy with a chain of cause-and-effect questions: nothing happens without a reason, and God does nothing without warning His prophets. Then he summons Israel's pagan enemies as witnesses to her oppression, and announces the tearing down of Bethel's altars and the mansions of the rich.
section.thesis: Election is not exemption but greater accountability; the God who reveals His purposes through His prophets has roared, and the judgment now falling on Israel's altars and ivory houses is neither random nor unannounced.
section.frameNote: Four movements: privilege that means greater accountability; the chain of inevitable cause and effect; foreign witnesses summoned against Israel; and the sentence on Bethel's altars and the great houses.

verses.text: Hear this word that the LORD has spoken against you, O children of Israel, against the whole family that I brought up out of the land of Egypt:
“Only you have I {{yada|known}} from all the families of the earth; therefore I will punish you for all your iniquities.”

units.label: Cause and effect
units.note: the lion has roared, who will not fear

verses.text: Can two walk together without agreeing where to go?
Does a lion roar in the forest when he has no prey? Does a young lion growl in his den if he has caught nothing?
Does a bird land in a snare where no bait has been set? Does a trap spring from the ground when it has nothing to catch?
If a ram’s horn sounds in a city, do the people not tremble? If calamity comes to a city, has not the LORD caused it?
Surely the Lord GOD does nothing without revealing His plan to His servants the prophets.
The lion has {{shaag|roared}}, who will not fear? The Lord GOD has spoken, who will not prophesy?
Proclaim to the citadels of Ashdod and to the citadels of Egypt: “Assemble on the mountains of Samaria; see the great unrest in the city and the acts of oppression in her midst.”
“For they know not how to do right,” declares the LORD. “They store up violence and destruction in their citadels.”
Therefore this is what the Lord GOD says: “An enemy will surround the land; he will pull down your strongholds and plunder your citadels.”

This is what the LORD says: “As the shepherd snatches from the mouth of the lion two legs or a piece of an ear, so the Israelites dwelling in Samaria will be rescued having just the corner of a bed or the cushion of a couch.

units.label: Bethel and the ivory houses fall
units.note: the horns of the altar cut off

verses.text: Hear and testify against the house of Jacob, declares the Lord GOD, the God of Hosts.
On the day I punish Israel for their transgressions, I will visit destruction on the altars of Bethel; the horns of the altar will be cut off, and they will fall to the ground.
I will tear down the winter house along with the summer house; the houses of ivory will also perish, and the great houses will come to an end,” declares the LORD.

yada.gloss: To know, but with far more than intellectual knowledge; it can mean to choose, to enter into intimate covenant relationship with. When God says only you have I known of all the families of the earth, He means Israel alone was set in covenant love. The shocking logic follows: such intimacy means greater, not lesser, accountability.

shaag.gloss: To roar, the sound of a lion over its prey. The shepherd Amos uses it to explain his own boldness: when the lion roars, fear is the only sane response, and when the Lord GOD speaks, prophesying is unavoidable. The roar that opened the book, Amos 1:2, now becomes the reason the prophet cannot keep silent.

basic.context: Amos begins the sermons of judgment by defending why he must speak. He reasons like a shepherd: effects have causes. A lion roars because it has prey; a trap springs because something touched it. So when disaster comes on a city, God is behind it, and He always warns His prophets first. Then Amos calls even Israel's pagan enemies to look at the oppression in Samaria, and announces that the altars of Bethel and the luxury houses of the rich will be torn down.
basic.hermeneutics: The key idea is that being chosen makes Israel more accountable, not less. God's special relationship with them is a reason for judgment when they rebel, not an excuse. The chain of questions defends the prophetic word: Amos speaks because God has spoken; the roar leaves him no choice.
basic.originalLanguages: God says only you have I known, yada, a word for intimate covenant choosing, and draws the surprising conclusion: therefore I will punish you. Amos explains his boldness with the lion's roar, shaag: when the lion roars you fear, and when the Lord GOD speaks you prophesy. The prophet cannot stay silent.
basic.typology: The prophet who must speak because God has spoken foreshadows Christ, the final Prophet and Word made flesh, and the whole line of witnesses God raises to call His people back. The horns of the altar cut off point to the futility of false worship and the need for the true altar and sacrifice.
basic.christ: Amos does nothing except relay what God reveals, pointing to Jesus, the Prophet who speaks only what the Father gives Him, and the Word Himself. The altars of Bethel, symbols of a corrupt, self-made religion, are torn down, clearing the way for the one true sacrifice offered by Christ, whose blood, unlike the horns of Bethel's altar, truly saves.
basic.god: God chooses in love but never winks at sin; intimacy with Him raises the standard. He is sovereign over history, behind even the calamities that fall on cities, yet He never acts without warning through His servants. And He is so committed to justice that He calls pagan nations to witness against His own people.
basic.crossReferences: Amos 3:7 is echoed wherever God reveals His plans to prophets. Luke 12:48 teaches that to whom much is given, much is required. Hebrews 1 shows God's final word spoken in His Son.
basic.oneStory: God's roar through Amos belongs to the long story of a God who warns before He judges and speaks before He acts, a story that climaxes when the Word Himself comes, and false altars give way to the one true sacrifice.

context.literary: The chapter is a tightly argued disputation. It opens with a covenant lawsuit summons, hear this word, then a string of rhetorical questions each demanding the answer no or of course, building to the punchline: the Lord GOD has spoken, who will not prophesy? The final section shifts to sentence, summoning witnesses and pronouncing doom on Bethel and the mansions.
context.historical: Bethel was the royal sanctuary of the northern kingdom, home to the golden calf worship instituted by Jeroboam I, 1 Kings 12. The horns of the altar were the projections at its corners, a place of refuge and the point where sacrificial blood was applied; cutting them off symbolized the end of any asylum or atonement there. Winter houses, summer houses, and houses of ivory reflect the luxury of Jeroboam II's elite, confirmed by ivory inlays excavated at Samaria.
context.canonical: The principle that election brings greater accountability, Amos 3:2, runs through Scripture to Jesus' teaching that much is required of those given much, Luke 12:48, and Peter's warning that judgment begins with the household of God, 1 Peter 4:17. Amos 3:7 is a foundational text on prophetic revelation, that God discloses His purposes to His servants the prophets.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To justify the prophetic word as the necessary echo of God's own voice, to overturn Israel's false security in election, and to announce that their corrupt worship and ill-gotten luxury will be torn down.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chain of questions describes how cause and effect and prophetic revelation work; it does not offer a formula for predicting every disaster. The abiding truths are God's sovereignty over history, His practice of warning before judging, and the heightened accountability of the privileged.

debates.question: Does Amos 3:6, has not the LORD caused it, make God the author of all calamity and even evil?

views.label: Direct sovereign causation
views.case: In the context of covenant judgment, the calamity in view is the disciplinary disaster God brings on a rebellious city; nothing happens outside His rule, and He owns His acts of judgment.

views.label: Sovereignty without authoring evil
views.case: Scripture affirms God's total sovereignty yet distinguishes His righteous judgments from moral evil, which He never authors, James 1:13; the verse asserts that judgment is not random, not that God is the source of sin.

debates.bearing: Both guard God's sovereignty and His holiness. The comfort and the warning of the verse is that history is not chaos: God rules it, and His judgments are purposeful and just, never capricious and never sinful.

study.originalLanguages: The pivot is the verb yada, to know: only you have I known of all the families of the earth. This is covenant intimacy, the same word used for the deepest personal knowing, and from it God draws the jarring inference, therefore I will punish you. Privilege is not a shield; it is a summons. Amos then grounds his own compulsion to speak in the image of the lion: the lion has shaag, roared, so who will not fear? The Lord GOD has spoken, so who can refuse to prophesy? The roar that opened the book returns as the engine of the prophet's courage. The judgment lands on the qarnot, the horns of the altar of Bethel, the very points of asylum and atonement, now cut off and fallen.

typology.type: The prophet who must speak only what God reveals, 3:7-8
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the final Prophet and the Word made flesh, who speaks only what the Father gives Him, John 12:49, Hebrews 1:1-2.

typology.type: The horns of the altar of Bethel cut off, 3:14
typology.fulfillment: The end of false, self-made worship as refuge, pointing to the one true altar and sacrifice of Christ, Hebrews 13:10.

study.christ: Amos insists he speaks nothing of his own; he only relays what God reveals to His servants the prophets. This anticipates Jesus, who said He does nothing on His own but speaks just what the Father has taught Him, and who is Himself the final Word God has spoken. The cutting off of the horns of Bethel's altar declares that Israel's counterfeit worship offers no refuge and no atonement; it clears the ground for the true altar, the cross, where the blood of Christ does what Bethel's altar never could. The Lion who roared in judgment is the same Lord who would be led as a Lamb to that altar in our place.
study.god: God's love is covenantal and particular; He knew Israel as He knew no other nation. Yet that very intimacy raises the standard, so that election means accountability, not immunity. He is sovereign over the events of history, standing behind even the disasters that discipline a rebellious people, while never being the author of evil. He is committed enough to justice that He summons pagan Ashdod and Egypt to witness against His own people, and He never strikes without first speaking through His prophets.

crossReferences.note: To whom much is given, much will be required, the New Testament form of Amos 3:2's logic of privilege and accountability.
Judgment begins with the household of God, echoing that God's own people are held to account first.
Jesus speaks only what the Father commands, fulfilling the prophet who relays God's revealed word.
Jeroboam's golden-calf sanctuary at Bethel, the corrupt worship whose altars Amos says will fall.

study.oneStory: Amos speaks because God has spoken, and God speaks before He acts. This belongs to the whole biblical story of a God who warns in mercy before He judges in justice, sending prophet after prophet until He finally sends His Son. The tearing down of Bethel's false altar clears the way for the one altar that saves, so that judgment on counterfeit religion serves the coming of true worship in Christ.

quiz.q: What does God mean by only you have I known, therefore I will punish you?

options.text: Israel's unique covenant intimacy with God raises their accountability rather than exempting them.
options.note: Being chosen means greater responsibility, not immunity.

options.text: God knows everything about everyone, so no sin is hidden.
options.note: The word here is covenant choosing, not general omniscience.

options.text: God had a special relationship with Israel that made their rebellion worse.
options.note: Right about the special relationship; the sharp point is that it increases accountability.

options.text: God had abandoned Israel and no longer knew them.
options.note: The opposite: it is precisely because He knew them that He acts.

quiz.q: Why does Amos pile up questions about lions, traps, and trumpets?

options.text: To show that effects have causes, so his prophesying is the unavoidable echo of God having spoken.
options.note: The lion has roared, so the prophet must speak; nothing is random.

options.text: To display his knowledge of nature as a shepherd.
options.note: The images serve an argument, not a nature lesson.

options.text: To argue that disasters and warnings always have a reason behind them.
options.note: True, and the climax is that God's word compels the prophet to speak.

options.text: To entertain the crowd with riddles.
options.note: These are pointed arguments, not entertainment.

quiz.q: Why does God summon Ashdod and Egypt to look at Samaria?

options.text: Even pagan nations can see Israel's oppression is appalling, witnessing against God's own people.
options.note: The enemies become witnesses to Israel's injustice.

options.text: God wanted to form an alliance with them.
options.note: They are summoned as witnesses to judgment, not allies.

options.text: To shame Israel by comparison with outsiders.
options.note: Right about the shame; specifically they testify to the oppression in Samaria.

options.text: Because they were more righteous than Israel.
options.note: They are called as witnesses, not held up as righteous.

quiz.q: What is the significance of cutting off the horns of the altar at Bethel?

options.text: It declares that Israel's false worship offers no refuge or atonement, pointing to the need for the true altar in Christ.
options.note: The horns were the place of asylum and atonement; their fall ends counterfeit worship.

options.text: It was simply property damage to a building.
options.note: The horns carried deep religious meaning; this is judgment on false worship.

options.text: It ended the sanctuary that had been a place of asylum and sacrifice.
options.note: Correct about asylum and sacrifice; the deeper point is the futility of false worship and the need for Christ.

options.text: It restored proper worship at Bethel.
options.note: It destroys Bethel's worship rather than restoring it.

quiz.q: What does Amos 3:7 teach about how God works?

options.text: God does nothing without revealing His plan to His servants the prophets; He warns before He acts.
options.note: God speaks before He acts, in mercy and justice.

options.text: God keeps all His plans completely secret.
options.note: The verse says the opposite: He reveals His plans to the prophets.

options.text: God communicates His purposes through chosen messengers.
options.note: Right, and specifically He does nothing in judgment without first warning through them.

options.text: God only acts after human beings figure out His will.
options.note: God initiates and reveals; He does not wait on human discovery.

questions: God says being chosen made Israel more accountable, not less. Where are you tempted to treat God's grace to you as a license rather than a responsibility?
Amos could not keep silent once God had spoken. Is there truth God has made clear to you that you are avoiding speaking or obeying?
Even pagan nations could see the oppression in Samaria. What might outsiders honestly observe about the way you or your community treat the vulnerable?
The luxurious houses and false altars were both torn down. What comforts or forms of religion are you leaning on that cannot finally hold?
God always warns before He acts. How does His patience in speaking before judging move you to listen to Him today?

translationNotes.note: Known translates yada, which carries the sense of covenant choosing and intimate relationship, not mere awareness; a footnote tradition renders it chosen.
Where BSB prints an em dash after roared and spoken, it has been rendered as a comma per house style; the two clauses are parallel rhetorical questions.
The image of a shepherd salvaging two legs or an ear from a lion's mouth pictures a rescue so minimal it is really a proof of death; only scraps of Samaria's luxury will remain.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Amos 3:1-15. Poetic line fragments joined into running verse text; footnote markers omitted; em dashes rendered as commas per house style.
